title: Responses to immigration in the Gilded Age
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on responses to immigration in the Gilded Age.
topic_path: Responses to immigration in the Gilded Age | 6.9
Responses to immigration in the Gilded Age

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![Puck cartoon](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/20d56487527d273d0b46f1e92a57f7c3.jpg)

>*Cartoon showing Columbia stirring a bowl labeled "Citizenship" with a spoon labeled "Equal Rights". Figures representing many nations are in the bowl; a caricature of an Irish man jumps up yelling, waving a knife and a green flag.* C.J. Taylor, "The Mortar of Assimilation and the One Element that won't mix," [Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mortar_of_Assimilation_Citizenship_1889.jpg), 1889.

**The image most directly reflects which of the following developments during the late nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the escalation in efforts to help immigrants adopt American culture and values
the rise in anti-immigrant sentiment in response to mass immigration
the passage of laws limiting immigration from all European and Asian countries
the introduction of strict citizenship laws to prevent future immigration

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![Puck cartoon](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/20d56487527d273d0b46f1e92a57f7c3.jpg)

>*Cartoon showing Columbia stirring a bowl labeled "Citizenship" with a spoon labeled "Equal Rights". Figures representing many nations are in the bowl; a caricature of an Irish man jumps up yelling, waving a knife and a green flag.* C.J. Taylor, "The Mortar of Assimilation and the One Element that won't mix," [Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mortar_of_Assimilation_Citizenship_1889.jpg), 1889.

**Which of the following changes to the United States during the late nineteenth century most directly contributed to the development depicted in the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise in immigration from European countries like Ireland and Italy
the creation of political machines that influenced local politics
the lack of job opportunities after a financial panic in the United States
the emergence of a temperance movement that aimed to ban alcohol

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![Puck cartoon](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/20d56487527d273d0b46f1e92a57f7c3.jpg)

>*Cartoon showing Columbia stirring a bowl labeled "Citizenship" with a spoon labeled "Equal Rights". Figures representing many nations are in the bowl; a caricature of an Irish man jumps up yelling, waving a knife and a green flag.* C.J. Taylor, "The Mortar of Assimilation and the One Element that won't mix," [Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mortar_of_Assimilation_Citizenship_1889.jpg), 1889.

**Which of the following best describes the message in the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Most immigrants could adopt American culture, but Irish immigrants could not Americanize.
Most immigrants could not handle having the full rights and privileges of American citizenship.
Most immigrants maintained their previous cultural values and languages rather than adopting American culture.
Most immigrants from Europe faced little oppression, but Asian immigrants often faced discrimination.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “There is no room in this country for hyphenated Americanism. When I refer to hyphenated Americans, I do not refer to naturalized Americans. Some of the very best Americans I have ever known were naturalized Americans, Americans born abroad. But a hyphenated American is not an American at all. This is just as true of the man who puts ‘native’ before the hyphen as of the man who puts German or Irish or English or French before the hyphen. Americanism is a matter of the spirit and of the soul. Our allegiance must be purely to the United States.”

>-Source: Theodore Roosevelt, in an address delivered before the Knights of Columbus, 1915

**The excerpt best serves as evidence of which of the following trends during the Gilded Age and Progressive Era?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the increase in public debates over the assimilation of immigrants
the rise of nativist sentiment in response to the rise in immigration
the escalation of immigration from non-European countries
the growth of patriotism following an attack on the United States

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“There is no room in this country for hyphenated Americanism. When I refer to hyphenated Americans, I do not refer to naturalized Americans. Some of the very best Americans I have ever known were naturalized Americans, Americans born abroad. But a hyphenated American is not an American at all. This is just as true of the man who puts ‘native’ before the hyphen as of the man who puts German or Irish or English or French before the hyphen. Americanism is a matter of the spirit and of the soul. Our allegiance must be purely to the United States.”

>-Source: Theodore Roosevelt, in an address delivered before the Knights of Columbus, 1915

**The remarks in the excerpt were most likely given in response to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: increase in immigration from European and Asian countries to the United States
development of political machines that provided immigrants with welfare services
spread of an anti-Catholic movement following mass German and Irish immigration
establishment of American boarding schools aimed at assimilating immigrant children

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“There is no room in this country for hyphenated Americanism. When I refer to hyphenated Americans, I do not refer to naturalized Americans. Some of the very best Americans I have ever known were naturalized Americans, Americans born abroad. But a hyphenated American is not an American at all. This is just as true of the man who puts ‘native’ before the hyphen as of the man who puts German or Irish or English or French before the hyphen. Americanism is a matter of the spirit and of the soul. Our allegiance must be purely to the United States.”

>-Source: Theodore Roosevelt, in an address delivered before the Knights of Columbus, 1915

**Based on the excerpt, Roosevelt would also be most likely to support which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the establishment of settlement houses
the implementation of an immigrant quota
the formation of immigrant enclaves in cities
the creation of bilingual schools

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“The millionaires are a product of natural selection, acting on the whole body of men to pick out those who can meet the requirement of certain work to be done. In this respect, they are just like the great statesmen, or scientific men, or military men. It is because they are thus selected that wealth—both their own and that entrusted to them—aggregates under their hands. . . . They may fairly be regarded as the naturally selected agents of society for certain work.”

>-Source: William Graham Sumner, *The Challenge of Facts: and Other Essays,* 1914

**The perspective expressed in the excerpt most directly supported which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: limited government regulation of businesses or the economy
government interference in business to prevent unethical behavior
government measures to redistribute wealth and address poverty
limited government intervention in the affairs of other countries

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“The millionaires are a product of natural selection, acting on the whole body of men to pick out those who can meet the requirement of certain work to be done. In this respect, they are just like the great statesmen, or scientific men, or military men. It is because they are thus selected that wealth—both their own and that entrusted to them—aggregates under their hands. . . . They may fairly be regarded as the naturally selected agents of society for certain work.”

>-Source: William Graham Sumner, *The Challenge of Facts: and Other Essays,* 1914

**Arguments similar to those expressed in the excerpt were employed earlier to oppose which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the establishment of government programs to aid the poor
the increased immigration from European and Asian countries
the government’s commitment to a *laissez-faire* economic policy
the consolidation of smaller railroad lines by Cornelius Vanderbilt

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“The millionaires are a product of natural selection, acting on the whole body of men to pick out those who can meet the requirement of certain work to be done. In this respect, they are just like the great statesmen, or scientific men, or military men. It is because they are thus selected that wealth—both their own and that entrusted to them—aggregates under their hands. . . . They may fairly be regarded as the naturally selected agents of society for certain work.”

>-Source: William Graham Sumner, *The Challenge of Facts: and Other Essays,* 1914

**The ideas about wealth expressed in the excerpt are most consistent with which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Social Darwinism
Social Gospel
Americanization
laissez-faire

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “It seemed to me that Hull-House ought to be able to devise some educational enterprise, which should build a bridge between European and American experiences in such wise as to give them both more meaning and a sense of relation. I meditated that perhaps the power to see life as a whole is more needed in the immigrant quarter of a large city than anywhere else, and that the lack of power is the most fruitful source of misunderstanding between European immigrants and their children, as it is between them and their American neighbors: and why should that chasm between fathers and sons, yawning at the feet of each generation, be made so unnecessarily cruel and impassable to these bewildered immigrants?”

>-Source: Jane Addams, *Twenty Years at Hull-House: With Autobiographical Notes,* 1912

**Which of the following issues of the period was Addams most likely concerned with in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the assimilation of immigrants into American culture
the rise in income inequality for American workers
the surge in anti-Catholic sentiment among northerners
the lack of success of the public education movement

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “It seemed to me that Hull-House ought to be able to devise some educational enterprise, which should build a bridge between European and American experiences in such wise as to give them both more meaning and a sense of relation. I meditated that perhaps the power to see life as a whole is more needed in the immigrant quarter of a large city than anywhere else, and that the lack of power is the most fruitful source of misunderstanding between European immigrants and their children, as it is between them and their American neighbors: and why should that chasm between fathers and sons, yawning at the feet of each generation, be made so unnecessarily cruel and impassable to these bewildered immigrants?”

>-Source: Jane Addams, *Twenty Years at Hull-House: With Autobiographical Notes,* 1912

**According to the author, what was the purpose of settlement houses?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: to help immigrants adopt US culture and the English language
to educate the children of factory workers in government public schools
to send American teachers to underdeveloped countries
to spread Protestant Christianity to Catholic and Jewish immigrants

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “It seemed to me that Hull-House ought to be able to devise some educational enterprise, which should build a bridge between European and American experiences in such wise as to give them both more meaning and a sense of relation. I meditated that perhaps the power to see life as a whole is more needed in the immigrant quarter of a large city than anywhere else, and that the lack of power is the most fruitful source of misunderstanding between European immigrants and their children, as it is between them and their American neighbors: and why should that chasm between fathers and sons, yawning at the feet of each generation, be made so unnecessarily cruel and impassable to these bewildered immigrants?”

>-Source: Jane Addams, *Twenty Years at Hull-House: With Autobiographical Notes,* 1912

**People who shared Addams’ ideas at the time most typically sought to achieve their goals by doing which of the following? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: providing social services in immigrant neighborhoods
advocating for stricter immigration policies
developing tenement neighborhoods in major cities
creating a political party aimed at improving immigrant life
